title: Restoring a teak garden table
subtitle: Clean back weathered teak and restore colour and protection with oil
excerpt: How to restore a teak garden table that has gone grey from weathering: cleaning the surface, sanding with the grain to remove the grey layer, applying teak or Danish oil to restore colour, and maintaining the finish.

glossaryTerms.definition: The silver-grey patina that teak develops on outdoor exposure when the natural teak oils oxidise and the surface cells break down under UV radiation and rain. The greying is purely surface-deep: 0.5 to 1.0 mm of grey wood conceals sound golden-brown teak beneath. Sanding the grey layer back to bare wood and applying oil restores the original colour.

A penetrating oil finish of linseed, tung, and alkyd resin that soaks into bare wood rather than sitting on the surface. It cures to a non-sticky film inside the wood grain, feeding the timber and providing moderate water resistance. On teak, Danish oil restores the golden colour and slows the return of the grey weathered layer. Requires re-application annually on outdoor furniture.

content.text: Teak weathers to silver-grey outdoors within one or two seasons. The
is superficial: below it is sound, stable teak. Sanding back the grey layer and applying
restores the colour and extends the time before re-treatment is needed. Some owners prefer the silver-grey patina and apply no oil; this is also fine; weathered teak is structurally sound.

Scrub the table with warm water and a stiff brush to remove lichen, mould, and loose grey fibres. For heavy algae growth, a dilute solution of outdoor timber cleaner (available at garden centres) applied before scrubbing cuts through faster than water alone. Rinse well and allow the timber to dry completely; at least 24 hours in warm weather. Wet teak will not take oil.
Step 2, sand back the grey layer

Sand all surfaces with 80-grit sandpaper on the sanding block, working strictly with the grain. Use the card scraper on any flat panels to remove the grey layer efficiently without scratching across the grain. The grey colour disappears quickly: fresh golden teak appears beneath it within a few strokes. Finish with 120-grit to smooth the raised grain left by the coarser paper. Remove all sanding dust with a dry brush.

Apply a generous first coat of Danish oil with the paint brush, working it into the grain. Allow it to penetrate for 15 minutes, then wipe off the surplus with a clean cloth before it becomes tacky. If the wood is very dry and absorbent, a second application while the first is still wet ensures full penetration. Apply three coats in total, allowing four to six hours between coats.

Reapply a single maintenance coat of oil each spring before the outdoor season starts. Light sanding with 120-grit before the maintenance coat removes the previous winter's weathering and ensures the new oil penetrates well. If the table has been stored indoors over winter, clean it and apply a single coat without sanding.

attrs.symptom: Oil goes sticky and does not cure
attrs.cause: Too much oil applied and the surplus was not wiped off before it skinned over.
attrs.fix: Wipe down with a cloth dampened with white spirit to remove the uncured oil, allow to dry, and re-apply a thinner coat.

attrs.cause: Danish oil rags generate heat as the oil cures and can spontaneously combust if bundled together.
attrs.fix: Spread used oil rags flat outdoors to cure, or submerge them in water in a sealed metal container. Never fold or bundle them indoors.
